{{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:古四王神社本殿.jpg|thumb|古四王神社本殿]] | 例祭 = September 8 | 社格 = Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings | 創建 = 伝 {{ill|1570|ja|1570|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1570|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) | 本殿 = Single-bay shrine Irimoya style | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 26 | 緯度秒 = 17.1 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 29 | 経度秒 = 57.0 | 地図 = Japan Akita }} '''Koshio Shrine''' (古四王神社) is a shrine located in [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Daisen, Akita|Daisen City]] 大曲字古四王際. The main hall has been designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == History == According to old documents of the Togashi family, the head of the parish, it is said that the main hall of Koshio Shrine was established in {{ill|1570|ja|1570|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1570|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) when the feudal lord [[:en:Tozawa clan|Tozawa Clan]] appointed the Peacock Castle lord {{ill|Togashi Clan|ja|冨樫氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togashi Clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富樫氏}} (冨樫左衛門太郎勝家) as magistrate. In 1930 (Showa 5), during dismantling and repair carried out by the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]] (now the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology), an ink inscription reading “古川村　大工　甚兵衛” was discovered among the roof’s structural elements, revealing that the work was by the carpenter Jinbei from Hida, Hida City in present-day Gifu Prefecture.財団法人文化財建造物保存技術協会編『重要文化財　古四王神社本殿保存修理工事報告書』P4 In 1905 (Meiji 38), an investigation was conducted by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University) commissioned by the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and in 1908 (Meiji 41) the main hall was designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the {{ill|Cultural Properties Protection Law|ja|文化財保護法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cultural Properties Protection Law|qq=}}) based on the {{ill|Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|ja|古社寺保存法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|qq=}}. This was the first cultural property designated building in Akita Prefecture. [[ja:古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)]] == Deities and Origins of the Shrine == The enshrined deities are recorded in [[:en:Kojiki|Records of Ancient Matters]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], and include {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (Ōhiko-no-Mikoto), who is said to have subdued the Hokuriku region, along with five other deities ({{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}, {{ill|Tatemikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ}}, {{ill|Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|ja|八坂刀売命|1=Yasakatome|2=ja|3=八坂刀売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂刀売神}}, {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}). Shrines named “Koshio” are common along the Sea of Japan coast from the Chūbu region northward, and examples can also be written as “胡四王”, “腰王”, or “故将”. Several theories have been proposed regarding its name: * The “Kōshi King Theory” holds that the enshrined {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} was the king of Koshi (the old name for the Hokuriku region). * The “Shitennō-ji Theory” suggests that the four guardian deities of Shitennō-ji—a temple which, through syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, served as the protector of Akita Castle—later became corrupted into the name “Koshio.” The origin of the main shrine is based on the Kōshi King Theory. Within Akita Prefecture, aside from the main shrine, branches are enshrined at [[:en:Koshiō Shrine|Akita City Jinaichi]], in Nikaho City, Ogata Town, and in Kazuno City, Hachimantai. According to the ancient history researcher {{ill|Niino Naokichi|ja|新野直吉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niino Naokichi|qq=}}, it is speculated that the deity of Akitaura mentioned in the Nihon Shoki under the section on Emperor Saimei made contact with Abe Hirafu, who led an expedition to the north, and subsequently merged with the Hokuriku god of Koshi. Moreover, drawing on the myth of {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} subjugating Hokuriku, many visit the main shrine seeking success in competitive endeavors. == Cultural Properties == Within the shrine precincts, the main hall, the offertory hall, and the worship hall are the principal structures, with the main hall designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == Architectural Overview == "Preservation and Repair Records of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall" (compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association) * Designation Category: Important Cultural Property * Name = Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture) | Native Name: 古四王神社本殿 (Koshio Shrine Main Hall) * Number of Structures: 1 building * Designation Date: April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) * Construction Date: 1570 (Genka Year 1) * Structural Style: Single-bay shrine Irimoya style, with side entrance, one-bay worship hall, karahafu (curved gable) style, thatched with cypress bark * Scale: Horizontal beam span 2.545 [[:en:Metre|m]], rafter span 2.545 m == Characteristics of the Main Hall == Created in the late Muromachi period, the main hall employs materials such as cedar, pine, cypress, and chestnut in appropriate locations. It is an eclectic structure that integrates elements of traditional Japanese style, Zen (Tang) style, and Great Buddha (Indian) style, while also incorporating local regional characteristics. Detailed features include carvings of wisteria arabesque on the side of the worship hall and the one-piece wooden construction of the [[:en:Giboshi|Giboshi]], among other refined techniques.'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P7–13 Furthermore, whereas most shrines typically face south or east, Koshio Shrine often faces north, and its veneration has been linked to martial deities—with some suggesting a connection to ancient Ezo rule. At the time, Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University), an authority on art and architecture, exclaimed, “A rarity among rarities, an oddity among oddities,” and later the architectural historian {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}} (professor at Kyoto Imperial University) also praised it as “a world-class building transcending Japanese, Chinese, and Indian styles.”'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P13 == Events == The regular Shinto ceremonies include the annual festival on September 8, door-opening on May 8, and door-closing on December 8, with New Year’s events held from December 31 through New Year’s Day. During the annual festival, kagura (sacred dance), lion dance, sword dance, and the dedication of Bonten are performed, along with the display and light-up of the main hall. In addition, in January—coinciding with Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day—the Koshio Shrine Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day and Koshio Hall Firefighting Mochi Festival are held. During the fire prevention drills, local firefighters and students from Higashi-Omagari Elementary School actively participate, followed by events during the mochi festival such as rice planting in the snow, Tenpitsu-yaki, and mochi pounding. == Timeline == * 1570 (Genka Year 1) – Establishment of the Koshio Shrine Main Hall. * 1873 (Meiji 6) – Becomes a village shrine. * 1905 (Meiji 38) – Investigation by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]], commissioned by the Ministry of Education’s Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association. * April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) – Designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the Cultural Properties Protection Law) based on the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law (Notification No. 43 of the Ministry of Home Affairs); on December 17, Suwa Shrine, Mizuhanome Shrine, and Inari Shrine are merged. * July 1930 (Showa 5) – Main hall dismantled and repaired (with new Tamagaki and Heiden constructed). * 1935 (Showa 10) – Investigation by Dr. {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}}, professor at Kyoto University. * October 1955 (Showa 30) – Major roof repairs. * 1966 (Showa 41) – Designated as a Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings. * July 1982 (Showa 57) – Fire disaster prevention facility work on the main hall. * November 1983 (Showa 58) – Repairs to the main hall’s protective fence. * November 1986 (Showa 61) – Major roof repairs of the main hall. * 2002 (Heisei 14) – Preservation and repair of the main hall. * January 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Torii gate collapses due to strong winds (reconstructed in July).[https://www.sakigake.jp/news/article/20210711AK0014/ Koshio Shrine Torii in Daisen, reinforced foundation and reconstruction after January’s strong winds] - Akita Sakigake Shinpō, viewed July 12, 2021. == Major Cultural Properties and Tourism in Daisen City == * [[:en:Former Ikeda Family Gardens|Former Ikeda Family Garden]] (National {{ill|Scenic Site|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic Site|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) * [[:en:Hotta-no-saku|Haruta Sakuato]] (National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]) * {{ill|Former Hongō Family Residence|ja|旧本郷家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Hongō Family Residence|qq=}} ({{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|qq=}}) == References == * Report on the Preservation and Repair Work of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall, compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association, Koshio Shrine, 2003 * Kyokyokai and Daisen City, “Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine”, 2007 * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Daisen City Official Website – Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}} == External Links == * [http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/01_jinja/daisen_oomagari/16_kosiou.html Koshio Shrine] – Akita Prefecture Shrine Agency * [https://www.akitafan.com/archive/tourism/330 Koshio Shrine (こしおうじんじゃ)] – Akita Prefecture Tourism Federation * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] – Daisen City, Akita Prefecture {{Shinto Navigation}} {{citation needed|ja|古四王神社|version=103544830|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]] [[ja:古四王神社]] [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]]{{translated page|ja|古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)|version=97729838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Buildings in Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:History of Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Koshiō-jinja (Daisen, Akita)}}

Investigation by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]], commissioned by the Ministry of Education’s Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association. * April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) – Designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the Cultural Properties Protection Law) based on the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law (Notification No. 43 of the Ministry of Home Affairs); on December 17, Suwa Shrine, Mizuhanome Shrine, and Inari Shrine are merged. * July 1930 (Showa 5) – Main hall dismantled and repaired (with new Tamagaki and Heiden constructed). * 1935 (Showa 10) – Investigation by Dr. {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}}, professor at Kyoto University. * October 1955 (Showa 30) – Major roof repairs. * 1966 (Showa 41) – Designated as a Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings. * July 1982 (Showa 57) – Fire disaster prevention facility work on the main hall. * November 1983 (Showa 58) – Repairs to the main hall’s protective fence. * November 1986 (Showa 61) – Major roof repairs of the main hall. * 2002 (Heisei 14) – Preservation and repair of the main hall. * January 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Torii gate collapses due to strong winds (reconstructed in July).[https://www.sakigake.jp/news/article/20210711AK0014/ Koshio Shrine Torii in Daisen, reinforced foundation and reconstruction after January’s strong winds] - Akita Sakigake Shinpō, viewed July 12, 2021. == Major Cultural Properties and Tourism in Daisen City == * [[:en:Former Ikeda Family Gardens|Former Ikeda Family Garden]] (National {{ill|Scenic Site|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic Site|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) * [[:en:Hotta-no-saku|Haruta Sakuato]] (National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]) * {{ill|Former Hongō Family Residence|ja|旧本郷家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Hongō Family Residence|qq=}} ({{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|qq=}}) == References == * Report on the Preservation and Repair Work of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall, compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association, Koshio Shrine, 2003 * Kyokyokai and Daisen City, “Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine”, 2007 * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Daisen City Official Website – Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}} == External Links == * [http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/01_jinja/daisen_oomagari/16_kosiou.html Koshio Shrine] – Akita Prefecture Shrine Agency * [https://www.akitafan.com/archive/tourism/330 Koshio Shrine (こしおうじんじゃ)] – Akita Prefecture Tourism Federation * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] – Daisen City, Akita Prefecture {{Shinto Navigation}} {{citation needed|ja|古四王神社|version=103544830|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]] [[ja:古四王神社]] [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]]{{translated page|ja|古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)|version=97729838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Buildings in Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:History of Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Shrines in Akita Prefecture
revision.text:
{{複数の問題 |出典の明記=2020th year 1月30日 (木) 04:59 (UTC) |wikify=2020th year 1月30日 (木) 04:59 (UTC) }} {{infobox religious building | name = Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture) | native_name = 古四王神社 | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Daisen, Akita|Daisen City]]大曲字古四王際30 | 祭神 = {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}}
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
{{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}
{{ill|Tatemikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ}}
{{ill|Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|ja|八坂刀売命|1=Yasakatome|2=ja|3=八坂刀売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂刀売神}}
{{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:古四王神社本殿.jpg|thumb|古四王神社本殿]] | 例祭 = September 8 | 社格 = Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings | 創建 = 伝 {{ill|1570|ja|1570|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1570|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) | 本殿 = Single-bay shrine Irimoya style | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 26 | 緯度秒 = 17.1 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 29 | 経度秒 = 57.0 | 地図 = Japan Akita }} '''Koshio Shrine''' (古四王神社) is a shrine located in [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Daisen, Akita|Daisen City]] 大曲字古四王際. The main hall has been designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == History == According to old documents of the Togashi family, the head of the parish, it is said that the main hall of Koshio Shrine was established in {{ill|1570|ja|1570|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1570|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) when the feudal lord [[:en:Tozawa clan|Tozawa Clan]] appointed the Peacock Castle lord {{ill|Togashi Clan|ja|冨樫氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togashi Clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富樫氏}} (冨樫左衛門太郎勝家) as magistrate. In 1930 (Showa 5), during dismantling and repair carried out by the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]] (now the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology), an ink inscription reading “古川村　大工　甚兵衛” was discovered among the roof’s structural elements, revealing that the work was by the carpenter Jinbei from Hida, Hida City in present-day Gifu Prefecture.財団法人文化財建造物保存技術協会編『重要文化財　古四王神社本殿保存修理工事報告書』P4 In 1905 (Meiji 38), an investigation was conducted by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University) commissioned by the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and in 1908 (Meiji 41) the main hall was designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the {{ill|Cultural Properties Protection Law|ja|文化財保護法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cultural Properties Protection Law|qq=}}) based on the {{ill|Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|ja|古社寺保存法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|qq=}}. This was the first cultural property designated building in Akita Prefecture. [[ja:古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)]] == Deities and Origins of the Shrine == The enshrined deities are recorded in [[:en:Kojiki|Records of Ancient Matters]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], and include {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (Ōhiko-no-Mikoto), who is said to have subdued the Hokuriku region, along with five other deities ({{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}, {{ill|Tatemikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ}}, {{ill|Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|ja|八坂刀売命|1=Yasakatome|2=ja|3=八坂刀売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂刀売神}}, {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}). Shrines named “Koshio” are common along the Sea of Japan coast from the Chūbu region northward, and examples can also be written as “胡四王”, “腰王”, or “故将”. Several theories have been proposed regarding its name: * The “Kōshi King Theory” holds that the enshrined {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} was the king of Koshi (the old name for the Hokuriku region). * The “Shitennō-ji Theory” suggests that the four guardian deities of Shitennō-ji—a temple which, through syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, served as the protector of Akita Castle—later became corrupted into the name “Koshio.” The origin of the main shrine is based on the Kōshi King Theory. Within Akita Prefecture, aside from the main shrine, branches are enshrined at [[:en:Koshiō Shrine|Akita City Jinaichi]], in Nikaho City, Ogata Town, and in Kazuno City, Hachimantai. According to the ancient history researcher {{ill|Niino Naokichi|ja|新野直吉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niino Naokichi|qq=}}, it is speculated that the deity of Akitaura mentioned in the Nihon Shoki under the section on Emperor Saimei made contact with Abe Hirafu, who led an expedition to the north, and subsequently merged with the Hokuriku god of Koshi. Moreover, drawing on the myth of {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} subjugating Hokuriku, many visit the main shrine seeking success in competitive endeavors. == Cultural Properties == Within the shrine precincts, the main hall, the offertory hall, and the worship hall are the principal structures, with the main hall designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == Architectural Overview == "Preservation and Repair Records of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall" (compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association) * Designation Category: Important Cultural Property * Name = Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture) | Native Name: 古四王神社本殿 (Koshio Shrine Main Hall) * Number of Structures: 1 building * Designation Date: April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) * Construction Date: 1570 (Genka Year 1) * Structural Style: Single-bay shrine Irimoya style, with side entrance, one-bay worship hall, karahafu (curved gable) style, thatched with cypress bark * Scale: Horizontal beam span 2.545 [[:en:Metre|m]], rafter span 2.545 m == Characteristics of the Main Hall == Created in the late Muromachi period, the main hall employs materials such as cedar, pine, cypress, and chestnut in appropriate locations. It is an eclectic structure that integrates elements of traditional Japanese style, Zen (Tang) style, and Great Buddha (Indian) style, while also incorporating local regional characteristics. Detailed features include carvings of wisteria arabesque on the side of the worship hall and the one-piece wooden construction of the [[:en:Giboshi|Giboshi]], among other refined techniques.'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P7–13 Furthermore, whereas most shrines typically face south or east, Koshio Shrine often faces north, and its veneration has been linked to martial deities—with some suggesting a connection to ancient Ezo rule. At the time, Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University), an authority on art and architecture, exclaimed, “A rarity among rarities, an oddity among oddities,” and later the architectural historian {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}} (professor at Kyoto Imperial University) also praised it as “a world-class building transcending Japanese, Chinese, and Indian styles.”'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P13 == Events == The regular Shinto ceremonies include the annual festival on September 8, door-opening on May 8, and door-closing on December 8, with New Year’s events held from December 31 through New Year’s Day. During the annual festival, kagura (sacred dance), lion dance, sword dance, and the dedication of Bonten are performed, along with the display and light-up of the main hall. In addition, in January—coinciding with Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day—the Koshio Shrine Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day and Koshio Hall Firefighting Mochi Festival are held. During the fire prevention drills, local firefighters and students from Higashi-Omagari Elementary School actively participate, followed by events during the mochi festival such as rice planting in the snow, Tenpitsu-yaki, and mochi pounding. == Timeline == * 1570 (Genka Year 1) – Establishment of the Koshio Shrine Main Hall. * 1873 (Meiji 6) – Becomes a village shrine. * 1905 (Meiji 38) – Investigation by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]], commissioned by the Ministry of Education’s Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association. * April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) – Designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the Cultural Properties Protection Law) based on the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law (Notification No. 43 of the Ministry of Home Affairs); on December 17, Suwa Shrine, Mizuhanome Shrine, and Inari Shrine are merged. * July 1930 (Showa 5) – Main hall dismantled and repaired (with new Tamagaki and Heiden constructed). * 1935 (Showa 10) – Investigation by Dr. {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}}, professor at Kyoto University. * October 1955 (Showa 30) – Major roof repairs. * 1966 (Showa 41) – Designated as a Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings. * July 1982 (Showa 57) – Fire disaster prevention facility work on the main hall. * November 1983 (Showa 58) – Repairs to the main hall’s protective fence. * November 1986 (Showa 61) – Major roof repairs of the main hall. * 2002 (Heisei 14) – Preservation and repair of the main hall. * January 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Torii gate collapses due to strong winds (reconstructed in July).[https://www.sakigake.jp/news/article/20210711AK0014/ Koshio Shrine Torii in Daisen, reinforced foundation and reconstruction after January’s strong winds] - Akita Sakigake Shinpō, viewed July 12, 2021. == Major Cultural Properties and Tourism in Daisen City == * [[:en:Former Ikeda Family Gardens|Former Ikeda Family Garden]] (National {{ill|Scenic Site|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic Site|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) * [[:en:Hotta-no-saku|Haruta Sakuato]] (National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]) * {{ill|Former Hongō Family Residence|ja|旧本郷家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Hongō Family Residence|qq=}} ({{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|qq=}}) == References == * Report on the Preservation and Repair Work of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall, compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association, Koshio Shrine, 2003 * Kyokyokai and Daisen City, “Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine”, 2007 * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Daisen City Official Website – Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}} == External Links == * [http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/01_jinja/daisen_oomagari/16_kosiou.html Koshio Shrine] – Akita Prefecture Shrine Agency * [https://www.akitafan.com/archive/tourism/330 Koshio Shrine (こしおうじんじゃ)] – Akita Prefecture Tourism Federation * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] – Daisen City, Akita Prefecture {{Shinto Navigation}} {{citation needed|ja|古四王神社|version=103544830|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]] [[ja:古四王神社]] [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]]{{translated page|ja|古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)|version=97729838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Buildings in Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:History of Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Koshiō-jinja (Daisen, Akita)}}

Several theories have been proposed regarding its name: * The “Kōshi King Theory” holds that the enshrined {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} was the king of Koshi (the old name for the Hokuriku region). * The “Shitennō-ji Theory” suggests that the four guardian deities of Shitennō-ji—a temple which, through syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, served as the protector of Akita Castle—later became corrupted into the name “Koshio.” The origin of the main shrine is based on the Kōshi King Theory. Within Akita Prefecture, aside from the main shrine, branches are enshrined at [[:en:Koshiō Shrine|Akita City Jinaichi]], in Nikaho City, Ogata Town, and in Kazuno City, Hachimantai. According to the ancient history researcher {{ill|Niino Naokichi|ja|新野直吉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niino Naokichi|qq=}}, it is speculated that the deity of Akitaura mentioned in the Nihon Shoki under the section on Emperor Saimei made contact with Abe Hirafu, who led an expedition to the north, and subsequently merged with the Hokuriku god of Koshi. Moreover, drawing on the myth of {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} subjugating Hokuriku, many visit the main shrine seeking success in competitive endeavors. == Cultural Properties == Within the shrine precincts, the main hall, the offertory hall, and the worship hall are the principal structures, with the main hall designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == Architectural Overview == "Preservation and Repair Records of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall" (compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association) * Designation Category: Important Cultural Property * Name = Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture) | Native Name: 古四王神社本殿 (Koshio Shrine Main Hall) * Number of Structures: 1 building * Designation Date: April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) * Construction Date: 1570 (Genka Year 1) * Structural Style: Single-bay shrine Irimoya style, with side entrance, one-bay worship hall, karahafu (curved gable) style, thatched with cypress bark * Scale: Horizontal beam span 2.545 [[:en:Metre|m]], rafter span 2.545 m == Characteristics of the Main Hall == Created in the late Muromachi period, the main hall employs materials such as cedar, pine, cypress, and chestnut in appropriate locations. It is an eclectic structure that integrates elements of traditional Japanese style, Zen (Tang) style, and Great Buddha (Indian) style, while also incorporating local regional characteristics. Detailed features include carvings of wisteria arabesque on the side of the worship hall and the one-piece wooden construction of the [[:en:Giboshi|Giboshi]], among other refined techniques.'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P7–13 Furthermore, whereas most shrines typically face south or east, Koshio Shrine often faces north, and its veneration has been linked to martial deities—with some suggesting a connection to ancient Ezo rule. At the time, Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University), an authority on art and architecture, exclaimed, “A rarity among rarities, an oddity among oddities,” and later the architectural historian {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}} (professor at Kyoto Imperial University) also praised it as “a world-class building transcending Japanese, Chinese, and Indian styles.”'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P13 == Events == The regular Shinto ceremonies include the annual festival on September 8, door-opening on May 8, and door-closing on December 8, with New Year’s events held from December 31 through New Year’s Day. During the annual festival, kagura (sacred dance), lion dance, sword dance, and the dedication of Bonten are performed, along with the display and light-up of the main hall. In addition, in January—coinciding with Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day—the Koshio Shrine Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day and Koshio Hall Firefighting Mochi Festival are held. During the fire prevention drills, local firefighters and students from Higashi-Omagari Elementary School actively participate, followed by events during the mochi festival such as rice planting in the snow, Tenpitsu-yaki, and mochi pounding. == Timeline == * 1570 (Genka Year 1) – Establishment of the Koshio Shrine Main Hall. * 1873 (Meiji 6) – Becomes a village shrine. * 1905 (Meiji 38) – Investigation by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]], commissioned by the Ministry of Education’s Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association. * April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) – Designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the Cultural Properties Protection Law) based on the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law (Notification No. 43 of the Ministry of Home Affairs); on December 17, Suwa Shrine, Mizuhanome Shrine, and Inari Shrine are merged. * July 1930 (Showa 5) – Main hall dismantled and repaired (with new Tamagaki and Heiden constructed). * 1935 (Showa 10) – Investigation by Dr. {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}}, professor at Kyoto University. * October 1955 (Showa 30) – Major roof repairs. * 1966 (Showa 41) – Designated as a Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings. * July 1982 (Showa 57) – Fire disaster prevention facility work on the main hall. * November 1983 (Showa 58) – Repairs to the main hall’s protective fence. * November 1986 (Showa 61) – Major roof repairs of the main hall. * 2002 (Heisei 14) – Preservation and repair of the main hall. * January 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Torii gate collapses due to strong winds (reconstructed in July).[https://www.sakigake.jp/news/article/20210711AK0014/ Koshio Shrine Torii in Daisen, reinforced foundation and reconstruction after January’s strong winds] - Akita Sakigake Shinpō, viewed July 12, 2021. == Major Cultural Properties and Tourism in Daisen City == * [[:en:Former Ikeda Family Gardens|Former Ikeda Family Garden]] (National {{ill|Scenic Site|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic Site|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) * [[:en:Hotta-no-saku|Haruta Sakuato]] (National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]) * {{ill|Former Hongō Family Residence|ja|旧本郷家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Hongō Family Residence|qq=}} ({{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|qq=}}) == References == * Report on the Preservation and Repair Work of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall, compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association, Koshio Shrine, 2003 * Kyokyokai and Daisen City, “Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine”, 2007 * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Daisen City Official Website – Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}} == External Links == * [http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/01_jinja/daisen_oomagari/16_kosiou.html Koshio Shrine] – Akita Prefecture Shrine Agency * [https://www.akitafan.com/archive/tourism/330 Koshio Shrine (こしおうじんじゃ)] – Akita Prefecture Tourism Federation * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] – Daisen City, Akita Prefecture {{Shinto Navigation}} {{citation needed|ja|古四王神社|version=103544830|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]] [[ja:古四王神社]] [[sn:Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture)]]{{translated page|ja|古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)|version=97729838|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Buildings in Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:History of Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Koshiō-jinja (Daisen, Akita)}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q85877700]] Wikidata name: Koshiō-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{複数の問題 |出典の明記=2020th year 1月30日 (木) 04:59 (UTC) |wikify=2020th year 1月30日 (木) 04:59 (UTC) }} {{infobox religious building | name = Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture) | native_name = 古四王神社 | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Daisen, Akita|Daisen City]]大曲字古四王際30 | 祭神 = {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}}
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}
{{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}
{{ill|Tatemikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ}}
{{ill|Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|ja|八坂刀売命|1=Yasakatome|2=ja|3=八坂刀売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂刀売神}}
{{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:古四王神社本殿.jpg|thumb|古四王神社本殿]] | 例祭 = September 8 | 社格 = Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings | 創建 = 伝 [[:en:1570|1570]] ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) | 本殿 = Single-bay shrine Irimoya style | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 26 | 緯度秒 = 17.1 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 29 | 経度秒 = 57.0 | 地図 = Japan Akita }} '''Koshio Shrine''' (古四王神社) is a shrine located in [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Daisen, Akita|Daisen City]] 大曲字古四王際. The main hall has been designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == History == According to old documents of the Togashi family, the head of the parish, it is said that the main hall of Koshio Shrine was established in [[:en:1570|1570]] ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) when the feudal lord [[:en:Tozawa clan|Tozawa Clan]] appointed the Peacock Castle lord {{ill|Togashi Clan|ja|冨樫氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togashi Clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富樫氏}} (冨樫左衛門太郎勝家) as magistrate. In 1930 (Showa 5), during dismantling and repair carried out by the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]] (now the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology), an ink inscription reading “古川村　大工　甚兵衛” was discovered among the roof’s structural elements, revealing that the work was by the carpenter Jinbei from Hida, Hida City in present-day Gifu Prefecture.財団法人文化財建造物保存技術協会編『重要文化財　古四王神社本殿保存修理工事報告書』P4 In 1905 (Meiji 38), an investigation was conducted by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University) commissioned by the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and in 1908 (Meiji 41) the main hall was designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the {{ill|Cultural Properties Protection Law|ja|文化財保護法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cultural Properties Protection Law|qq=}}) based on the {{ill|Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|ja|古社寺保存法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|qq=}}. This was the first cultural property designated building in Akita Prefecture. [[ja:古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)]] == Deities and Origins of the Shrine == The enshrined deities are recorded in [[:en:Kojiki|Records of Ancient Matters]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], and include {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (Ōhiko-no-Mikoto), who is said to have subdued the Hokuriku region, along with five other deities ({{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}, {{ill|Tatemikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ}}, {{ill|Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|ja|八坂刀売命|1=Yasakatome|2=ja|3=八坂刀売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂刀売神}}, {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}). Shrines named “Koshio” are common along the Sea of Japan coast from the Chūbu region northward, and examples can also be written as “胡四王”, “腰王”, or “故将”. Several theories have been proposed regarding its name: * The “Kōshi King Theory” holds that the enshrined {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} was the king of Koshi (the old name for the Hokuriku region). * The “Shitennō-ji Theory” suggests that the four guardian deities of Shitennō-ji—a temple which, through syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, served as the protector of Akita Castle—later became corrupted into the name “Koshio.” The origin of the main shrine is based on the Kōshi King Theory. Within Akita Prefecture, aside from the main shrine, branches are enshrined at [[:en:Koshiō Shrine|Akita City Jinaichi]], in Nikaho City, Ogata Town, and in Kazuno City, Hachimantai. According to the ancient history researcher {{ill|Niino Naokichi|ja|新野直吉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niino Naokichi|qq=}}, it is speculated that the deity of Akitaura mentioned in the Nihon Shoki under the section on Emperor Saimei made contact with Abe Hirafu, who led an expedition to the north, and subsequently merged with the Hokuriku god of Koshi. Moreover, drawing on the myth of {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} subjugating Hokuriku, many visit the main shrine seeking success in competitive endeavors. == Cultural Properties == Within the shrine precincts, the main hall, the offertory hall, and the worship hall are the principal structures, with the main hall designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == Architectural Overview == "Preservation and Repair Records of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall" (compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association) * Designation Category: Important Cultural Property * Name = Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture) | Native Name: 古四王神社本殿 (Koshio Shrine Main Hall) * Number of Structures: 1 building * Designation Date: April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) * Construction Date: 1570 (Genka Year 1) * Structural Style: Single-bay shrine Irimoya style, with side entrance, one-bay worship hall, karahafu (curved gable) style, thatched with cypress bark * Scale: Horizontal beam span 2.545 [[:en:Metre|m]], rafter span 2.545 m == Characteristics of the Main Hall == Created in the late Muromachi period, the main hall employs materials such as cedar, pine, cypress, and chestnut in appropriate locations. It is an eclectic structure that integrates elements of traditional Japanese style, Zen (Tang) style, and Great Buddha (Indian) style, while also incorporating local regional characteristics. Detailed features include carvings of wisteria arabesque on the side of the worship hall and the one-piece wooden construction of the [[:en:Giboshi|Giboshi]], among other refined techniques.'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P7–13 Furthermore, whereas most shrines typically face south or east, Koshio Shrine often faces north, and its veneration has been linked to martial deities—with some suggesting a connection to ancient Ezo rule. At the time, Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University), an authority on art and architecture, exclaimed, “A rarity among rarities, an oddity among oddities,” and later the architectural historian {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}} (professor at Kyoto Imperial University) also praised it as “a world-class building transcending Japanese, Chinese, and Indian styles.”'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P13 == Events == The regular Shinto ceremonies include the annual festival on September 8, door-opening on May 8, and door-closing on December 8, with New Year’s events held from December 31 through New Year’s Day. During the annual festival, kagura (sacred dance), lion dance, sword dance, and the dedication of Bonten are performed, along with the display and light-up of the main hall. In addition, in January—coinciding with Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day—the Koshio Shrine Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day and Koshio Hall Firefighting Mochi Festival are held. During the fire prevention drills, local firefighters and students from Higashi-Omagari Elementary School actively participate, followed by events during the mochi festival such as rice planting in the snow, Tenpitsu-yaki, and mochi pounding. == Timeline == * 1570 (Genka Year 1) – Establishment of the Koshio Shrine Main Hall. * 1873 (Meiji 6) – Becomes a village shrine. * 1905 (Meiji 38) – Investigation by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]], commissioned by the Ministry of Education’s Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association. * April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) – Designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the Cultural Properties Protection Law) based on the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law (Notification No. 43 of the Ministry of Home Affairs); on December 17, Suwa Shrine, Mizuhanome Shrine, and Inari Shrine are merged. * July 1930 (Showa 5) – Main hall dismantled and repaired (with new Tamagaki and Heiden constructed). * 1935 (Showa 10) – Investigation by Dr. {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}}, professor at Kyoto University. * October 1955 (Showa 30) – Major roof repairs. * 1966 (Showa 41) – Designated as a Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings. * July 1982 (Showa 57) – Fire disaster prevention facility work on the main hall. * November 1983 (Showa 58) – Repairs to the main hall’s protective fence. * November 1986 (Showa 61) – Major roof repairs of the main hall. * 2002 (Heisei 14) – Preservation and repair of the main hall. * January 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Torii gate collapses due to strong winds (reconstructed in July).[https://www.sakigake.jp/news/article/20210711AK0014/ Koshio Shrine Torii in Daisen, reinforced foundation and reconstruction after January’s strong winds] - Akita Sakigake Shinpō, viewed July 12, 2021. == Major Cultural Properties and Tourism in Daisen City == * [[:en:Former Ikeda Family Gardens|Former Ikeda Family Garden]] (National {{ill|Scenic Site|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic Site|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) * [[:en:Hotta-no-saku|Haruta Sakuato]] (National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]) * {{ill|Former Hongō Family Residence|ja|旧本郷家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Hongō Family Residence|qq=}} ({{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|qq=}}) == References == * Report on the Preservation and Repair Work of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall, compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association, Koshio Shrine, 2003 * Kyokyokai and Daisen City, “Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine”, 2007 * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Daisen City Official Website – Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}} == External Links == * (こしおうじんじゃ)] – Akita Prefecture Tourism Federation * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:古四王神社本殿.jpg|thumb|古四王神社本殿]] | 例祭 = September 8 | 社格 = Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings | 創建 = 伝 [[:en:1570|1570]] ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) | 本殿 = Single-bay shrine Irimoya style | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 26 | 緯度秒 = 17.1 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 29 | 経度秒 = 57.0 | 地図 = Japan Akita }} '''Koshio Shrine''' (古四王神社) is a shrine located in [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Daisen, Akita|Daisen City]] 大曲字古四王際. The main hall has been designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == History == According to old documents of the Togashi family, the head of the parish, it is said that the main hall of Koshio Shrine was established in [[:en:1570|1570]] ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) when the feudal lord [[:en:Tozawa clan|Tozawa Clan]] appointed the Peacock Castle lord {{ill|Togashi Clan|ja|冨樫氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togashi Clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富樫氏}} (冨樫左衛門太郎勝家) as magistrate. In 1930 (Showa 5), during dismantling and repair carried out by the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]] (now the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology), an ink inscription reading “古川村　大工　甚兵衛” was discovered among the roof’s structural elements, revealing that the work was by the carpenter Jinbei from Hida, Hida City in present-day Gifu Prefecture.財団法人文化財建造物保存技術協会編『重要文化財　古四王神社本殿保存修理工事報告書』P4 In 1905 (Meiji 38), an investigation was conducted by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University) commissioned by the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association of the [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and in 1908 (Meiji 41) the main hall was designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the {{ill|Cultural Properties Protection Law|ja|文化財保護法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cultural Properties Protection Law|qq=}}) based on the {{ill|Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|ja|古社寺保存法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|qq=}}. This was the first cultural property designated building in Akita Prefecture. [[ja:古四王神社 (秋田県大仙市)]] == Deities and Origins of the Shrine == The enshrined deities are recorded in [[:en:Kojiki|Records of Ancient Matters]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], and include {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (Ōhiko-no-Mikoto), who is said to have subdued the Hokuriku region, along with five other deities ({{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}, {{ill|Tatemikoto|ja|建御名方命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミナカタ}}, {{ill|Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|ja|八坂刀売命|1=Yasakatome|2=ja|3=八坂刀売命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka-Tomachinomikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂刀売神}}, {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}). Shrines named “Koshio” are common along the Sea of Japan coast from the Chūbu region northward, and examples can also be written as “胡四王”, “腰王”, or “故将”. Several theories have been proposed regarding its name: * The “Kōshi King Theory” holds that the enshrined {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} was the king of Koshi (the old name for the Hokuriku region). * The “Shitennō-ji Theory” suggests that the four guardian deities of Shitennō-ji—a temple which, through syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, served as the protector of Akita Castle—later became corrupted into the name “Koshio.” The origin of the main shrine is based on the Kōshi King Theory. Within Akita Prefecture, aside from the main shrine, branches are enshrined at [[:en:Koshiō Shrine|Akita City Jinaichi]], in Nikaho City, Ogata Town, and in Kazuno City, Hachimantai. According to the ancient history researcher {{ill|Niino Naokichi|ja|新野直吉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niino Naokichi|qq=}}, it is speculated that the deity of Akitaura mentioned in the Nihon Shoki under the section on Emperor Saimei made contact with Abe Hirafu, who led an expedition to the north, and subsequently merged with the Hokuriku god of Koshi. Moreover, drawing on the myth of {{ill|Oohiko no Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oohiko no Mikoto|qq=}} subjugating Hokuriku, many visit the main shrine seeking success in competitive endeavors. == Cultural Properties == Within the shrine precincts, the main hall, the offertory hall, and the worship hall are the principal structures, with the main hall designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. == Architectural Overview == "Preservation and Repair Records of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall" (compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association) * Designation Category: Important Cultural Property * Name = Koshio Shrine (Daisen City, Akita Prefecture) | Native Name: 古四王神社本殿 (Koshio Shrine Main Hall) * Number of Structures: 1 building * Designation Date: April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) * Construction Date: 1570 (Genka Year 1) * Structural Style: Single-bay shrine Irimoya style, with side entrance, one-bay worship hall, karahafu (curved gable) style, thatched with cypress bark * Scale: Horizontal beam span 2.545 [[:en:Metre|m]], rafter span 2.545 m == Characteristics of the Main Hall == Created in the late Muromachi period, the main hall employs materials such as cedar, pine, cypress, and chestnut in appropriate locations. It is an eclectic structure that integrates elements of traditional Japanese style, Zen (Tang) style, and Great Buddha (Indian) style, while also incorporating local regional characteristics. Detailed features include carvings of wisteria arabesque on the side of the worship hall and the one-piece wooden construction of the [[:en:Giboshi|Giboshi]], among other refined techniques.'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P7–13 Furthermore, whereas most shrines typically face south or east, Koshio Shrine often faces north, and its veneration has been linked to martial deities—with some suggesting a connection to ancient Ezo rule. At the time, Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]] (professor at Tokyo Imperial University), an authority on art and architecture, exclaimed, “A rarity among rarities, an oddity among oddities,” and later the architectural historian {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}} (professor at Kyoto Imperial University) also praised it as “a world-class building transcending Japanese, Chinese, and Indian styles.”'Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine' P13 == Events == The regular Shinto ceremonies include the annual festival on September 8, door-opening on May 8, and door-closing on December 8, with New Year’s events held from December 31 through New Year’s Day. During the annual festival, kagura (sacred dance), lion dance, sword dance, and the dedication of Bonten are performed, along with the display and light-up of the main hall. In addition, in January—coinciding with Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day—the Koshio Shrine Cultural Property Fire Prevention Day and Koshio Hall Firefighting Mochi Festival are held. During the fire prevention drills, local firefighters and students from Higashi-Omagari Elementary School actively participate, followed by events during the mochi festival such as rice planting in the snow, Tenpitsu-yaki, and mochi pounding. == Timeline == * 1570 (Genka Year 1) – Establishment of the Koshio Shrine Main Hall. * 1873 (Meiji 6) – Becomes a village shrine. * 1905 (Meiji 38) – Investigation by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]], commissioned by the Ministry of Education’s Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association. * April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) – Designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the Cultural Properties Protection Law) based on the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law (Notification No. 43 of the Ministry of Home Affairs); on December 17, Suwa Shrine, Mizuhanome Shrine, and Inari Shrine are merged. * July 1930 (Showa 5) – Main hall dismantled and repaired (with new Tamagaki and Heiden constructed). * 1935 (Showa 10) – Investigation by Dr. {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}}, professor at Kyoto University. * October 1955 (Showa 30) – Major roof repairs. * 1966 (Showa 41) – Designated as a Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings. * July 1982 (Showa 57) – Fire disaster prevention facility work on the main hall. * November 1983 (Showa 58) – Repairs to the main hall’s protective fence. * November 1986 (Showa 61) – Major roof repairs of the main hall. * 2002 (Heisei 14) – Preservation and repair of the main hall. * January 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Torii gate collapses due to strong winds (reconstructed in July).[https://www.sakigake.jp/news/article/20210711AK0014/ Koshio Shrine Torii in Daisen, reinforced foundation and reconstruction after January’s strong winds] - Akita Sakigake Shinpō, viewed July 12, 2021. == Major Cultural Properties and Tourism in Daisen City == * [[:en:Former Ikeda Family Gardens|Former Ikeda Family Garden]] (National {{ill|Scenic Site|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic Site|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) * [[:en:Hotta-no-saku|Haruta Sakuato]] (National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]) * {{ill|Former Hongō Family Residence|ja|旧本郷家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Hongō Family Residence|qq=}} ({{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|qq=}}) == References == * Report on the Preservation and Repair Work of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall, compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association, Koshio Shrine, 2003 * Kyokyokai and Daisen City, “Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine”, 2007 * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Daisen City Official Website – Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}} == External Links == * [http://akita-jinjacho.sakura.ne.jp/tatsujin_etc/01_jinja/daisen_oomagari/16_kosiou.html Koshio Shrine] – Akita Prefecture Shrine Agency * [https://www.akitafan.com/archive/tourism/330 Koshio Shrine (こしおうじんじゃ)] – Akita Prefecture Tourism Federation * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] – Daisen City, Akita Prefecture {{Shinto Navigation}} {{citation needed|ja|古四王神社|version=103544830|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} history}} [[Category:Buildings in Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:History of Daisen, Akita]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|ミズハノメ|lt=Water Wave Goddess|lt_ja=水波女神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = File:古四王神社本殿.jpg | 例祭 = September 8 | 社格 = Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings | 創建 = 伝 [[:en:1570|1570]] ([[:en:Genki (era)|Genka]] Year 1) | 本殿 = Single-bay shrine Irimoya style | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 26 | 緯度秒 = 17.1 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 29 | 経度秒 = 57.0 | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture = {{ill|Daisen|qid=Q695920}} | coordinates = {{Coord|39.438083|N|140.499167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Koshio Shrine''' (古四王神社) is a shrine located in [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Daisen, Akita|Daisen City]] 大曲字古四王際. This was the first cultural property designated building in Akita Prefecture. Investigation by Dr. [[:en:Itō Chūta|Itō Chūtā]], commissioned by the Ministry of Education’s Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Association. * April 23, 1908 (Meiji 41) – Designated as a “Special Protected Building” (equivalent to an “Important Cultural Property” under the Cultural Properties Protection Law) based on the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law (Notification No. 43 of the Ministry of Home Affairs); on December 17, Suwa Shrine, Mizuhanome Shrine, and Inari Shrine are merged. * July 1930 (Showa 5) – Main hall dismantled and repaired (with new Tamagaki and Heiden constructed). * 1935 (Showa 10) – Investigation by Dr. {{ill|Amenuma Shun'ichi|ja|天沼俊一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenuma Shun'ichi|qq=}}, professor at Kyoto University. * October 1955 (Showa 30) – Major roof repairs. * 1966 (Showa 41) – Designated as a Shrine of Divine Food and Cloth Offerings. * July 1982 (Showa 57) – Fire disaster prevention facility work on the main hall. * November 1983 (Showa 58) – Repairs to the main hall’s protective fence. * November 1986 (Showa 61) – Major roof repairs of the main hall. * 2002 (Heisei 14) – Preservation and repair of the main hall. * January 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Torii gate collapses due to strong winds (reconstructed in July).[https://www.sakigake.jp/news/article/20210711AK0014/ Koshio Shrine Torii in Daisen, reinforced foundation and reconstruction after January’s strong winds] - Akita Sakigake Shinpō, viewed July 12, 2021. == Major Cultural Properties and Tourism in Daisen City == * [[:en:Former Ikeda Family Gardens|Former Ikeda Family Garden]] (National {{ill|Scenic Site|ja|名勝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Scenic Site|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) * [[:en:Hotta-no-saku|Haruta Sakuato]] (National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]) * {{ill|Former Hongō Family Residence|ja|旧本郷家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Hongō Family Residence|qq=}} ({{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|qq=}}) == References == * Report on the Preservation and Repair Work of the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine Main Hall, compiled by the Cultural Property Construction Preservation Technology Association, Koshio Shrine, 2003 * Kyokyokai and Daisen City, “Regional Treasure: Volunteer Guide Manual Born from a Citizen Study Group on the Important Cultural Property, Koshio Shrine”, 2007 * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Daisen City Official Website – Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}} == External Links == * (こしおうじんじゃ)] – Akita Prefecture Tourism Federation * [http://www.city.daisen.akita.jp/docs/2013110600112/ Nationally Designated Registered Tangible Cultural Property (Building) “Koshio Shrine Main Hall”] – Daisen City, Akita Prefecture {{Shinto Navigation}} {{citation needed|ja|古四王神社|version=103544830|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }}
